title: Objects inside Arrays 🧱
description: Combine arrays and objects to mimic real-world databases.

slides.title: The Ultimate Structure 🦾
slides.content:
# The Real World

We know arrays are great for storing lists. We know objects are great for storing detailed data about a single thing.

What happens when we need a **list** of **detailed things**?

Welcome to real-world database architecture! 90% of all data on the internet is passed around as **Arrays of Objects**.

Let's picture building a Twitter feed. You don't have one user. You have an Array (a list) of many Users (objects)!

slides.title: Database Schema 🗺️
slides.content:
## Building the Array of Objects

```javascript
let allUsers = [
  { name: "Alice", age: 25 },
  { name: "Bob", age: 34 },
  { name: "Charlie", age: 19 }
];
```

Let's break this down:
1. `allUsers` is a standard Array `[]`.
2. Inside the Array, there are 3 items, separated by commas.
3. Each one of those items is an Object `{}` with Keys and Values.

Now we can store nearly infinite amounts of beautifully organized data!

slides.title: Accessing the Architecture 🏗️
slides.content:
## Walking the Path

How do we get Bob's age? We read the structure from the outside in!

1. Target the Array variable: `allUsers`
2. Target Bob's specific index in the array: `[1]`
3. Inside the target object, use Dot Notation to target the key: `.age`

```javascript
console.log( allUsers[1].age ); // Outputs: 34

console.log( allUsers[0].name ); // Outputs: "Alice"
```

slides.title: Try It Out 💻
slides.content: Try changing the code below to print out Charlie's score instead of Alice's!

quizData.question: What is the proper code to access the 'make' of the second car inside this array? let cars = [ {make: 'Ford'}, {make: 'Chevy'} ];
quizData.explanation: Arrays are zero-indexed, so the second car is at Index 1 (cars[1]). Once inside the object, use dot notation (.make) to grab the specific value.

slides.title: Chapter Summary! 🎉
slides.content:
## You Finished Chapter 7!

You can now handle massive blocks of data!

You've learned:

* How to store lists in **Arrays** `[ ]` using Zero-Based Indexes.
* Adding and removing data using `.push()` and `.pop()`
* Iterating over lists quickly using `.forEach()` and `.map()`
* Creating perfectly labelled data pieces using **Objects** `{ }`
* Combining both to build real-world Array/Object data structures!

Up next: Chapter 8, where we explore manipulating text via a deep dive on Strings! 🧵
